title: AASEG: ATTENTION AWARE NETWORK FOR REAL TIME SEMANTIC SEGMENTATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## AASEG: ATTENTION AWARE NETWORK FOR REAL TIME SEMANTIC SEGMENTATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we present a new network named Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic image segmentation. Our network incorporates spatial and channel information using Spatial Attention (SA) and Channel Attention (CA) modules respectively. It also uses dense local multi-scale context information using Multi Scale Context (MSC) module. The feature maps are concatenated individually to produce the final segmentation map. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method using a comprehensive analysis, quantitative experimental results and ablation study using Cityscapes, ADE20K and Camvid datasets. Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Semantic Segmentation is a fundamental problem in computer vision where the goal is to label each and every pixel in the image to its appropriate class. Since it is required to be deployed in real world settings like robots and autonomous vehicles, hence there is a need to balance the speed vs performance tradeoff. Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) (Long et al., 2015) composed of convolutional layers was one of the first works to get strong semantic representation.

However, this method was not able to capture boundary information accurately. Atrous convolutions (Yu and Koltun, 2015) at the last several stages of their network was used to give feature maps with strong semantic representation, thus solving the problem with FCN based architectures. However, this comes at at the cost of increased computational complexity. For real world navigation tasks like autonomous driving, there is a need to improve the Frame Per Second(FPS).

Chen et al. (2017) and (Yu and Koltun, 2015) used dilated convolutions to increase the recpetive field while maintaining the number of parameters. SegNet (Badrinarayanan et al., 2017) utilizes a small network structure and the skip-connected method to achieve improved FPS. (Mehta et al., 2018), (Paszke et al., 2016) and (Poudel et al., 2019) proposed unique approaches to tackle real time semantic segmentation problem.

## 2 RELATED WORK

(Fu et al., 2019) introduces spatial-wise and channelwise attention modules to enhance the recpetive field (Paszke et al., 2016) trims a a lot of convolution filters to reduce computation. ICNet (Zhao et al., 2018a) proposed an image cascade network using multiresolution branches. (Iandola et al., 2016) allows the neural network to find the critical channels of the feature map and select the most suitable channels by itself.

ESPNet (Mehta et al., 2018) introduces an efficient spatial pyramid (ESP), which brings great improvement in both speed and performance. Bilateral Segmentation Network (BiSeNet) (Yu et al., 2018a) used two parts: Spatial Path (SP) is used to get with the loss of spatial information and Context Path (CP) for compressing the receptive field. (Yu et al., 2020) used multi-path framework to combine the low-level details and high-level semantics.

(Li et al., 2019b) utilizes a light-weight backbone to speed up its network and a multi scale feature aggregation to improve accuracy. SwiftNet (Orsic et al., 2019) used lateral connections to restore

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

the prediction resolution while maintaining the speed. (Lin et al., 2017) uses a multipath refinement network to refine the feature but ignores the global context feature.
The speed-Accuracy performance comparison of state of the art methods on the Cityscapes test set is shown in Figure 1:
Figure 1: Speed-Accuracy performance comparison on the Cityscapes test set. Our approach achieves higher mean IOU while still being faster than most existing methods.

.

We summarize our main contributions as follows:

• We propose a novel Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic segmentation.

• Our network is comprised of three parts: Spatial Attention(SA) module to capture the spatial dependencies of the feature maps, Channel Attention(CA) module to extract high level semantic information and Multi Scale Context(MSC) module to learn the information flow between feature maps of consecutive levels.

• Detailed experiments and analysis indicate the efficacy of our proposed network in not only improving the performance but also FPS. We achieve results on par with previous state of the art using Cityscapes, Camvid and on ADE20K datasets.

## 3 BACKGROUND

## 3.1 SPATIAL INFORMATION

The spatial information of the image is important to predict the detailed output for semantic segmentation. Modern existing approaches uses encoding of spatial information. DUC (Wang et al., 2018), PSPNet (Zhao et al., 2017), DeepLab v2 (Chen et al., 2017) use the dilated convolution to preserve the spatial size of the feature map.

## 3.2 CONTEXT INFORMATION

Semantic segmentation requires context information to generate a high-quality result. Most of the used methods enlarge the receptive field or fuse different contextual information. (Chen et al., 2017), (Wang et al., 2018) and (Yu and Koltun, 2015) use different dilation rates in convolution layers to capture different scale contextual information. In (Chen et al., 2017), an “ASPP” module is used to capture context information of different receptive field. PSPNet (Zhao et al., 2017) applies a “PSP” module which contains several different scales of average pooling layers.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 3.3 ATTENTION MECHANISM

(Hu et al., 2018) applied channel attention for image recognition and achieve the state-of-the-art. (Yu et al., 2018b) proposed a network that learns the global context as attention and revise the features. Multi scale network along with a custom attention module was proposed by (Sagar and Soundrapandiyan, 2020) for semantic segmentation.

## 3.4 FEATURE FUSION

The spatial information captured by the Spatial Path consists of rich detailed information. The output feature of the Context Path is made up of contextual information. Feature fusion was used to achieve state of the art results on image classification, object detection and instance segmentation using DMSANet (Sagar, 2021b).

## 4 PROPOSED METHOD

## 4.1 DATASET

The following datasets have been used to benchmark our results:

**1. Cityscapes** It is used for urban street segmentation. The 5000 annotated images are used in our experiments which are divided into 2975, 500 and 1525 images for training, validation, and testing respectively.
**2. ADE20K** This dataset contains labels of 150 object categories. The dataset includes 20k,2k and 3k images for training, validation and testing respectively.
**3. CamVid** This dataset is used for semantic segmentation for autonomous driving scenarios. It is composed of 701 densely annotated images.

## 4.2 NETWORK ARCHITECTURE

The fundamental goal in semantic segmentation is to map an RGB image _X ∈ R[H][×][W][ ×]_[3] to a semantic map _Y ∈ R[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ with the same spatial resolution _H × W_ , where _C_ is the number of classes of objects present in image. The input image _X_ is converted to a set of feature maps _Fl_ where l=1,...,3 from each network stage, where _Fl ∈ R[H][l][W][l][C][l]_ is a _Cl_ -dimensional feature map.

Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures. The input image is first passed through a block comprising of convolutional, batch normalization and ReLU activation function.

We express a convolution layer _W[n]_ ( _x_ ) as follows:

where _⊙_ represents the convolution operator, _W[n][×][n]_ represents the _n × n_ convolutional kernel, _x_ represents the input data and _b_ represents the bias vector.

## 4.3 MULTI SCALE CONTEXT MODULE

The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 _×_ 1 convolution, 3 _×_ 3 convolution and 5 _×_ 5 convolution respectively. The individual feature maps are then fused together. The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 _×_ 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256. The feature map produced is of size _H × W × nc_ , where _H, W_ are the height and width of the feature map, and _nc_ denotes the number of channels.

The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. The dilated convolution layer input at every stage is formed by concatenating the input feature map with the outputs from previous convolutions. At the final step, the outputs from the three dilated convolutions are concatenated with the input feature map.

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 4.4 SPATIAL ATTENTION MODULE

The spatial attention module is used for capturing the spatial dependencies of the feature maps. The spatial attention (SA) module in our network is defined below:

where _W_ 1 and _W_ 2 denotes the first and second 1 _×_ 1 convolution layer respectively, _x_ denotes the input data, _fSigmoid_ denotes the sigmoid function, _fReLU_ denotes the ReLu activation function.

The spatial attention module used in this work is shown in Figure 2:

Figure 2: Details of our spatial attention module

.

## 4.5 CHANNEL ATTENTION MODULE

The channel attention module is used for extracting high level multi-scale semantic information. The channel attention (CA) module in our network is defined below:

where _W_ 1 and _W_ 2 denotes the first and second 1 _×_ 1 convolution layer, _x_ denotes the input data. _fAvgP ool_[1][denotes the global average pooling function,] _[ f][Sigmoid]_[ denotes the Sigmoid function,] _[ f][ReLU]_ denotes ReLU activation function.

The channel attention module used in this work is shown in Figure 3:

Figure 3: Details of our channel attention module

.

## 4.6 AGGREGATION

We denote the concatenation operation as follows:

where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator and _x_ 1, _x_ 2 and _x_ 3 represents the features of the two branches. The AASeg module can be denoted as follow:
_xAASeg_ = (( _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fCA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fMSC_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ )) (5)

where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator, _fCA_ represents the channel attention module mentioned in Equation 2, _fSA_ represents the spatial attention module, _fMSC_ represents the multi scale attention and _xconcat_ represents the combined feature.
we use _ConvXi_ to denote the operations of _i[th]_ block. Therefore, the output of _i[th]_ block is calculated as follows:

where _ConvX_ includes one of each convolutional layer, batch normalization layer and ReLU activation layer, _ki_ is the kernel size of convolutional layer, _xi−_ 1 and _xi_ are the input and output of _i[th]_ block. Fusion operation is used to combine high-level features with low-level features using Equation 7:

The overall structure of our proposed AASeg network is shown in Figure 4.

Figure 4: Architecture of proposed AASeg network architecture. “c” denotes concatenation.

.

## 4.7 LOSS FUNCTIONS

We use cross-entropy loss function to weigh the difference between the forward propagation result of the network and the ground truth of the samples. The cross-entropy loss is calculated as defined in Equation 8:

where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
We also use the auxiliary supervision loss _Laux_ to improve the model performance thus making it easier to optimize. The auxiliary loss can be defined as:

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

where _B_ is the mini batch size, _N_ is the number of pixels in every batch; _K_ is the number of categories; _pijk_ is the prediction of the _j[th]_ pixel in the _i[th]_ sample for the _k[th]_ class, _I_ ( _gij_ = _k_ ) is a function which is defined in Equation 10.
The class attention loss _Lcls_ from channel attention module is also used. The class attention loss is
where _aijk_ is the value generated of the class attention map of the _j[th]_ pixel in the _i[th]_ sample for the _k[th]_ class.

where _λ_ 1, _λ_ 2 and _λ_ 3 are set as 1, 0.5 and 0.5 to balance the loss.

## 4.8 IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS

PyTorch deep learning framework is used to carry out our experiments. Our neural networks is trained with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) as optimizer, batch size of 16, momentum of 0.9 and weight decay of 5 _e[−]_[4] . The network is trained for 20K iterations with an initial learning rate value of 0.01. The, “poly” learning rate policy is used to decay the initial learning rate while training the model to reduce over-fitting.
Data augmentation operations like random horizontal flip, random resizing with scale range of [1.0, 2.0], and random cropping with crop size of 1024 _×_ 1024 was done.

## 4.9 EVALUATION METRICS

For quantitative evaluation of the performance of our network, mean of class-wise intersection-overunion (mIoU) is used for bench-marking the performance, number of float-point operations (FLOPs) and frames per second (FPS) are used for bench-marking the speed.

## 5 RESULTS

We present the segmentation accuracy and inference speed of our proposed method on Cityscapes validation and test set in Table 1. We use the training set and validation set to train our models before submitting to Cityscapes online server.

The qualitative results on Cityscapes validation set are presented in Figure 5:

.

We evaluate our method on CamVid dataset. The comparison results with other methods is shown in Table 2:

The comparison of our network with previous state of the art methods for semantic segmentation using ADE20K validation set is shown in Table 3:
Table 3: Performance comparison with results reported on ADE20K validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.

## 6 ABLATION STUDY

We explore the effect of 3 different upsamling operations: bilinear upsampling, deconvolution and nearest neighbor upsampling as shown in Table 4. The best results are obtained using bilinear upsampling but there isn’t considerable difference.
Table 4: Ablation study on Upsampling operation in our network using Cityscapes validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.

|old.||
|---|---|
|Method|mIoU (%)|
|bilinear upsampling|**79.2**|
|deconvolution|78.5|
|nearest neighbor|78.3|
We also try the various kernel size in Table 5. Larger kernel size of 7 _×_ 7 is also tried which results in a similar performance but increases computational complexity.

We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3. It seems large dilation rates increases the performance but comes at the cost of increased number of parameters, hence decrease in FPS. This effect is shown in Table 6:

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Table 5: Ablation study on kernel size k in our network using Cityscapes validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.
Table 6: Ablation study results on Cityscapes validation set. FPS are estimated for an input image of resolution of 512 _×_ 1024. Best results are highlighted in bold.

## 7 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we present a new network named Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic segmentation. We use Spatial Attention(SA) and Channel Attention(CA) modules to enhance features of objects without adding extra computational cost. The fused spatial-channel attention along with Multi Scale Context(MSC) module enables network to extract discriminative and robust features of targets or background. We test our network using Cityscapes, ADE20K and Camvid datasets. Our network balances speed-performance tradeoff at par with previous state of the art network architectures. In the future, we would like to use multi scale attention module for instance segmentation problem.

## ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

We would like to thank Nvidia for providing the GPUs.

This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.label: Zero Shot (claude-opus-4.7)
zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper presents AASeg, a real-time semantic segmentation network combining spatial attention, channel attention, and multi-scale context modules. The paper has significant issues including missing equation content (many equations are referenced but show as blank/missing from the extracted text), inconsistencies between the ablation study numbers and the main results table, vague architectural descriptions, and unsubstantiated claims about the MSC module. Some core formulas and modules are under-specified, and several numerical values appear inconsistent across tables.

comments.title: Mean IoU inconsistency between main results and ablation
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports AASeg achieves 74.8% mIoU on the Cityscapes validation set. However, the ablation tables (Tables 4, 5, 6) report AASeg-baseline and variants achieving 78.3-80.2% mIoU on the same Cityscapes validation set. These numbers are mutually inconsistent — if the baseline already achieves 79.2%, how does the main comparison report only 74.8%? This is a major inconsistency that undermines the credibility of the reported results.

comments.title: MSC module description is internally inconsistent
comments.quote: The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 _×_ 1 convolution, 3 _×_ 3 convolution and 5 _×_ 5 convolution respectively... The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12.
comments.explanation: The MSC section first describes splitting the feature map into three parallel convolutions (1×1, 3×3, 5×5) and fusing them. Then it describes a completely different operation: dilated convolutions with rates 3, 6, 12 applied sequentially with concatenation. It is unclear whether these are two sequential stages of the MSC module or two alternative descriptions. The architectural design of a core contribution is thus underspecified and cannot be reproduced from the description.

comments.title: Dilation rate ablation inconsistent with method description
comments.quote: We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3.
comments.explanation: The method section (Sec 4.3) states the dilated convolutions use dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. But the ablation says 'increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3' and Table 6 shows AASeg-(r=1), (r=2), (r=3) variants. This is inconsistent with the method description and leaves the actual network configuration unclear.

comments.title: Channel reduction from 2048 without backbone is unexplained
comments.quote: The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 _×_ 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: The authors claim AASeg uses no backbone (stated explicitly: 'Our network dosen't use any backbone to extract features'). Yet the MSC module takes a feature map with 2048 channels as input. Where do 2048 channels come from if there is no backbone? A single convolutional block followed by BN and ReLU would not typically produce 2048 channels from an RGB input. This makes the architecture difficult to reproduce and the 'no backbone' claim suspect.

comments.title: MSC module equation missing / described as attention inconsistently
comments.quote: _fMSC_ represents the multi scale attention
comments.explanation: Earlier the MSC module is defined as 'Multi Scale Context (MSC)' module, but in Equation 5's description it's referred to as 'multi scale attention'. This naming inconsistency, combined with the ambiguous module description, makes the role of MSC unclear. Also, there is no formal equation for f_MSC comparable to the SA and CA equations, whereas these others have explicit formulas.

comments.title: Equation 5 concatenation with element-wise multiplication is ambiguous
comments.quote: _xAASeg_ = (( _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fCA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fMSC_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ))
comments.explanation: The ⊗ operator is introduced without definition. Presumably it denotes element-wise (channel-wise or spatial-wise) multiplication, but the dimensions of SA output, CA output and MSC output with x_concat must be compatible. Standard SA produces an H×W×1 map (multiplied spatially), CA produces a 1×1×C vector (multiplied channel-wise), and MSC would produce a full H×W×C feature map. The operator ⊗ cannot mean the same thing in all three cases — this key detail about how modules are combined is not specified.

comments.title: Three contributions described but text refers to 'two branches'
comments.quote: where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator and _x_ 1, _x_ 2 and _x_ 3 represents the features of the two branches.
comments.explanation: The text defines three variables x_1, x_2, x_3 but describes them as 'features of the two branches'. This is inconsistent — there are three branches (SA, CA, MSC), not two. This is a small but concrete inconsistency in the formal definitions.

comments.title: Duplicate rows in CamVid results table
comments.explanation: Table 2 on CamVid contains the two SFNet rows listed twice (exactly duplicated). This is likely a typographical error but it reflects carelessness in result reporting.

comments.title: Overstated performance claim in abstract
comments.quote: Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.
comments.explanation: Looking at Table 1, AASeg's test mIoU of 74.4% is surpassed by BiSeNetV1 (ResNet18) at 74.7%, SFNet at 74.5%, BiSeNetV2-L at 75.3%, STDC1-Seg75 at 75.3%, and STDC2-Seg75 at 76.8%. Meanwhile STDC1-Seg50 runs faster (250.4 vs 202.7 FPS). So AASeg is neither the most accurate nor the fastest. Calling it 'better than most previous architectures' in both speed and accuracy is an overclaim that depends heavily on what is meant by 'better'.

comments.title: Cross-entropy loss formula for binary case used without justification
comments.quote: where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: The description is of binary cross-entropy, but semantic segmentation is a multi-class problem (C classes per pixel, as defined in Sec 4.2 with Y ∈ R^(H×W×C)). The standard loss is categorical cross-entropy summed over pixels and classes. The description does not match the actual loss used for a C-class segmentation problem. This is either incorrect or highly misleading.

comments.title: Missing equation content throughout
comments.quote: We express a convolution layer _W[n]_ ( _x_ ) as follows:
comments.explanation: Multiple equations (for convolution, SA, CA, aggregation, i-th block output, fusion, cross-entropy loss, auxiliary loss, class attention loss, and total loss) are referenced in the text but the actual equation content is not present in the extracted document. While this may be an OCR issue, several of these equations are critical to understanding the methodology, and the text descriptions alone leave many details ambiguous (e.g., how SA and CA differ structurally, what the fusion operation is, etc.). This severely limits reproducibility.

comments.title: ADE20K table has two SFNet rows without distinguishing information
comments.explanation: Table 3 lists two SFNet entries with different mIoU and GFLOPs values but no distinguishing backbone or resolution. The reader cannot tell what the difference between these two entries is, making a proper comparison impossible.

comments.title: Inconsistency: AASeg 'no backbone' but MSC outputs 2048 channels
comments.quote: Our network dosen't use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures.
comments.explanation: Combined with the earlier point about 2048 channels, this statement conflicts with the implied architecture. A single conv-BN-ReLU block from a 3-channel input will not produce 2048 channels. Either an actual backbone is used (contradicting this claim) or the 2048 figure is wrong. Either way, the architecture section is inconsistent.

zero_shot__gpt-5.5.label: Zero Shot (gpt-5.5)
zero_shot__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important practical problem and combines familiar ingredients for real-time segmentation: lightweight convolutional feature extraction, spatial/channel attention, multi-scale context, and auxiliary losses. However, several core methodological details are either internally inconsistent or too underspecified to reproduce reliably. The largest concerns are the unexplained discrepancy between the reported Cityscapes validation numbers in the main comparison and the ablation tables, the inconsistent description of dilation rates and their parameter cost, and the insufficiently specified architecture despite the claim of using no backbone. Some loss definitions and the role of the channel-attention/class-attention loss also appear inconsistent with standard multiclass semantic segmentation formulations. These issues make it difficult to assess whether the reported speed-accuracy tradeoff is supported by the described method.

comments.title: Architecture is not reproducibly specified, and the 2048-channel feature source is unclear
comments.quote:
Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures. The input image is first passed through a block comprising of convolutional, batch normalization and ReLU activation function.

The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 _×_ 1 convolution, 3 _×_ 3 convolution and 5 _×_ 5 convolution respectively. The individual feature maps are then fused together. The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 _×_ 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: I first read the no-backbone claim as meaning that the paper defines a complete lightweight encoder itself, which is a reasonable design choice for real-time segmentation. I then looked for the missing channel schedule, number of blocks, strides/downsampling stages, and exact fusion layout. The text only says that the image is passed through a convolution-BN-ReLU block, yet the MSC description later assumes a fused feature map with 2048 channels. In standard segmentation architectures, 2048 channels usually comes from a deep backbone stage such as ResNet-50/101, so its appearance here is surprising for a network claimed to have no backbone and only about 0.76M--0.90M parameters in the ablation table. The figures may give a visual overview, but the written method does not specify enough layer widths, repetitions, strides, or tensor sizes to derive where 2048 channels come from or to reproduce the network.

comments.title: Contradictory dilation-rate descriptions between MSC and ablation
comments.quote: The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12.
comments.explanation: The MSC module description is clear at first: it uses three dilated convolutions with rates 3, 6, and 12, which is consistent with common ASPP-like designs. However, the ablation section later says that the increasing sequence of dilation rates is 0, 1, 2, 3 and Table 6 reports variants labeled AASeg-(r=1), AASeg-(r=2), and AASeg-(r=3). I tried to interpret these as different experimental settings, but the paper does not explain how the rates 3/6/12 relate to the later sequence 0/1/2/3 or to the single parameter r. A reader cannot tell which dilation configuration was used for the main AASeg result.

comments.title: Dilation is incorrectly described as increasing the number of parameters
comments.quote: It seems large dilation rates increases the performance but comes at the cost of increased number of parameters, hence decrease in FPS.
comments.explanation: The speed decrease from larger effective receptive fields could be plausible if the authors also change the number of layers, branches, or channels. But as written, the claim attributes an increase in parameter count to larger dilation rates. For a standard convolution, changing the dilation rate changes the sampling pattern and receptive field but not the number of learned weights, which remains kernel_height × kernel_width × input_channels × output_channels. Table 6 also shows parameter counts increasing from r=1 to r=3, but that cannot be caused by dilation alone. The paper needs to clarify what architectural change accompanies r; otherwise the explanation is technically incorrect.

comments.title: MSC is treated as an attention mask in the aggregation equation, but described as a feature-producing context module
comments.quote:
_xAASeg_ = (( _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fCA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fMSC_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ )) (5)

where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator, _fCA_ represents the channel attention module mentioned in Equation 2, _fSA_ represents the spatial attention module, _fMSC_ represents the multi scale attention and _xconcat_ represents the combined feature.
comments.explanation: The spatial and channel attention terms naturally produce multiplicative weights that can be applied to xconcat, so the first two products are understandable. The MSC module, however, was described earlier as a multi-scale context block that applies 1×1/3×3/5×5 convolutions and dilated convolutions, then concatenates feature maps. That sounds like it outputs transformed features, not an attention map with the same shape and range as xconcat. Equation 5 instead multiplies fMSC(xconcat) elementwise by xconcat and even calls it 'multi scale attention'. This may be the intended design, but the paper never states how MSC is converted into a compatible gating tensor or whether the multiplication is actually intended. The mismatch affects the implementable definition of the core AASeg module.

comments.title: Concatenation notation refers to three inputs but calls them two branches
comments.quote: where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator and _x_ 1, _x_ 2 and _x_ 3 represents the features of the two branches.
comments.explanation: The intended operation is probably simple concatenation, and the surrounding architecture does use multiple branches, so this is not hard to guess. Still, the notation introduces three tensors x1, x2, and x3 while describing them as 'the features of the two branches.' Since the architecture already has SA, CA, and MSC paths, as well as earlier multi-scale convolution paths, the branch count matters for understanding which tensors are concatenated. This should be corrected to avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Cross-entropy loss description appears to use a binary formulation for a multiclass segmentation problem
comments.quote:
We use cross-entropy loss function to weigh the difference between the forward propagation result of the network and the ground truth of the samples. The cross-entropy loss is calculated as defined in Equation 8:


where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: Semantic segmentation here is explicitly multiclass, with C classes in the output map and K categories in the auxiliary loss. For multiclass pixel-wise segmentation, the usual loss is a sum over pixels and classes, e.g. -sum y_{ijk} log p_{ijk}, often averaged over valid pixels. The prose definition of yn as the probability that the result is true and 1-yn as false instead reads like binary cross-entropy, and it does not identify the predicted class probabilities, labels, pixels, or ignore-label handling. It is possible the omitted equation image contained the intended expression, but the accompanying definitions are inconsistent with the multiclass setting and leave the main training objective ambiguous.

comments.title: Class-attention loss is not reconciled with the channel-attention module definition
comments.quote:
The class attention loss _Lcls_ from channel attention module is also used. The class attention loss is 


where _aijk_ is the value generated of the class attention map of the _j[th]_ pixel in the _i[th]_ sample for the _k[th]_ class.
comments.explanation: I understand the motivation: auxiliary supervision on an attention map can encourage class-discriminative features. The problem is that the earlier Channel Attention module is described using global average pooling and 1×1 convolutions, which conventionally produces channel-wise weights, not a per-pixel, per-class map aijk. The paper does not explain how the channel-attention output is projected to K class maps at every pixel, whether aijk is before or after sigmoid/softmax, or how it aligns with ground-truth labels. Without that mapping, the class-attention loss cannot be implemented from the stated CA module.

comments.title: Cityscapes validation results in ablation tables conflict with the main comparison
comments.explanation: The main Cityscapes table reports AASeg at 74.8% validation mIoU and 202.7 FPS at 512×1024 resolution. Later, the upsampling ablation on the Cityscapes validation set reports 79.2% mIoU for bilinear upsampling, and Table 6 reports an AASeg-baseline of 79.2% mIoU but only 118.2 FPS, also for 512×1024 input. I checked whether the ablation might be on a different dataset split or resolution, but the table captions say Cityscapes validation set and 512×1024. A 4.4-point validation mIoU gap and a large FPS gap for what appears to be the same model/dataset setting is not a minor reporting difference; it undermines interpretation of both the main benchmark and the ablation study unless the training protocol or model variant differences are explicitly explained.

comments.title: FPS comparisons are not reproducible without hardware and inference protocol
comments.quote:
Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.

PyTorch deep learning framework is used to carry out our experiments.
comments.explanation: FPS is a central claim of the paper, and the tables compare methods primarily on speed-accuracy tradeoff. However, the implementation details do not specify the GPU model, CUDA/cuDNN versions, whether inference uses PyTorch eager mode, TorchScript, TensorRT, mixed precision, batch size, warm-up iterations, timing resolution, inclusion/exclusion of preprocessing and postprocessing, or whether all baselines were remeasured under the same setup. Real-time segmentation papers usually need these details because FPS can vary substantially across hardware and inference engines. The absence of this information makes the 202.7 FPS claim and comparisons to prior work difficult to reproduce or fairly assess.

comments.title: Claim of no extra computational cost from attention modules is overstated
comments.quote: We use Spatial Attention(SA) and Channel Attention(CA) modules to enhance features of objects without adding extra computational cost.
comments.explanation: The qualitative claim that the modules are lightweight is plausible: 1×1 convolutions, pooling, sigmoid activations, and elementwise products may add relatively little cost compared with the main convolutions. But they are not cost-free. The SA and CA definitions explicitly include additional convolutional layers and nonlinearities, and the aggregation concatenates multiple processed branches. Unless the authors mean 'without significant computational cost' and support it with FLOP/latency ablations, the statement overclaims what the architecture can provide.

comments.title: Ablation study does not isolate the claimed contributions of SA, CA, and MSC
comments.quote:
Detailed experiments and analysis indicate the efficacy of our proposed network in not only improving the performance but also FPS.

We explore the effect of 3 different upsamling operations: bilinear upsampling, deconvolution and nearest neighbor upsampling as shown in Table 4.
comments.explanation: The paper's main claimed contributions are the Spatial Attention, Channel Attention, and Multi Scale Context modules. I looked for ablations removing SA, CA, MSC, or combinations of them, because that is the standard way to support such component-level claims. The shown ablations instead vary upsampling method, kernel size, and dilation rate. Those are useful implementation studies, but they do not demonstrate that SA, CA, or MSC individually improve mIoU or speed. The claim that the experiments establish the efficacy of the proposed modules is therefore stronger than what is shown.
